symbols: bare torso marked by scourging — the body broken for the sins of the world, the Suffering Servant bearing stripes
loincloth — Christ stripped of all dignity, reduced to utter humiliation for humanity's sake
dark background — the spiritual darkness of evil engulfing the Light of the World
bound or restrained posture — the voluntary submission of the omnipotent God to human cruelty
averted gaze — sorrow and endurance, not defiance; the meekness of the Lamb led to slaughter
warm flesh tones against darkness — the humanity of Christ luminous even in suffering
description: A half-length portrait of Christ after the scourging. The figure emerges from deep darkness, his muscular torso bare except for a white cloth around the waist. His head, framed by reddish-brown hair and a full beard, is turned slightly to the left with a gaze of sorrowful endurance. The body shows the marks of flagellation. One arm is pulled back, suggesting restraint at a column or post behind him.
theological_description: Titian's Scourging of Christ presents the Passion with visceral intimacy, stripping away narrative context to force a direct encounter with the suffering Christ. The half-length format transforms the viewer into a witness standing immediately before the scourging post — there is no distance, no crowd to hide behind. This is the moment foretold by Isaiah: 'I gave my back to those who struck me' (Isaiah 50:6) and 'by his stripes we are healed' (Isaiah 53:5). The theological paradox is absolute: the one through whom all things were made (John 1:3) submits his body to destruction by his creatures. Titian's warm, luminous flesh against the enveloping darkness visualizes John's theological framework: the light shines in the darkness (John 1:5), and here the darkness appears to be winning — yet the very radiance of Christ's body proclaims otherwise. The bound posture expresses the voluntary nature of the Passion: Christ is not overpowered but chooses to remain bound, for 'no one takes my life from me; I lay it down of my own accord' (John 10:18). The averted gaze, sorrowful yet composed, fulfills the prophecy of the Lamb led to slaughter who does not open his mouth (Isaiah 53:7). Every brushstroke of suffering flesh is simultaneously a proclamation of redeeming love.

scripture_refs.reason: Pilate took Jesus and had him flogged — the direct event depicted
By his stripes we are healed — the Suffering Servant prophecy fulfilled in the scourging marks on Christ's body
I gave my back to those who struck me — the Servant's voluntary submission to beating
Like a lamb led to slaughter, he did not open his mouth — Christ's silent endurance visible in his composed expression
No one takes my life from me — the voluntary nature of Christ's suffering, choosing to remain bound
He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree, that by his wounds you have been healed
